narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a middle-class family background, you've put together a respectable profile for law school admission. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.5% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.7, signaling to the model an early struggle in your grades relative to peers. Next, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's stronger than most applicants', helping to offset some concerns. Your Year 3 decile of 2—still below the average of around 5.5—was the third biggest influence, again highlighting a dip in school rankings that nudged the model toward expecting bar exam challenges. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 32 falls short of the 36.7 average, and while being full-time aligns with over 90% of applicants, it didn't sway the outcome much here. These early and later academic deciles were the dominant drivers, outweighing your strengths and making the model view your bar passage odds as too risky for admission. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your above-average undergrad performance showing real potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic standing through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings. Building a narrative around your upward trajectory could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift the model's view and open new doors.
